content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHMRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
August 2,
1995
Honorable Stan Brauer
City of Loma Linda
11781 Welebir St.
Loma Linda,
CA
92354
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—95-229
Dear Councilmember Brauer:
This is in response to your letter requesting follow—up
advice regarding your responsibilities as a member of the Loma
Linda City Council under the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
As indicated in our
telephone conversation of July 25,
1995, your letter does not
address a particular decision before the city council but rather
asks about numerous decisions of the city council without specific
facts.
Accordingly,
we are treating your letter as a request for
informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation
18329,
and offer you only general guidance to your questions.
2
QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS
QUESTION 1.
By virtue of your personal residence that is located within
300 feet of the boundaries of the Hillside Initiative Area,
do you
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000
— 18995.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-95-229
Page2
have a conflict of interest with respect to the following
decisions of the Loma Linda City Council:
a.
Discussion concerning litigation .against
the city that has been filed alleging damages due
to the current hillside initiative?
b.
Decisions concerning possible changes to
the Hillside Initiative, including the vote on the
placement of a new referendum on the ballot to amend
or rescind the existing initiative?
c.
Decisions to implement specific grading
and building standards related to the current
initiative which require a 4/5 vote of the city?
CONCLUSION 1.
a.,
b
and c.
Because your property is within 300 feet of
the proposed Hillside Initiative Area,
you may not participate in
decision concerning the Hillside Initiative Area, unless it
will have no financial effect on your real property.
This would
include decisions concerning litigation on the existing
initiative, decisions concerning a possible referendum,
and
implementation decisions.
Generally, each decision should be
analyzed separately to determine if you have a conflict of
interest.
QUESTION 2.
Does it make a difference if you are only voting on
recommendations by the city’s planning commission to the City of
Loma Linda?
CONCLUSION 2.
-
-
No.
If you have a conflict of interest in a decision, you
are prohibited from using your official position with the city to
influence planning commission and city decisions.
You are
prohibited from participating or influencing the decision in city
council metings, for example,
no matter how th issue is raised.
Moreover,
you may not contact any members or employees of the
city, including the planning commission, to influence the
decision.
However,
you may address the city council with respect to
your personal interests.
Where this exception does apply, your
comments must be limited to your personal interests,
and care
should be taken to clarify that. you are not acting in any official
capacity.
Thus, for example, the exception would not permit you
to appear before the city council to represent any other person’s
interests or the interests of any group.


Our File No.
1-95-229
Page
3
QUESTION 3.
May.you express your views or address the public on your
views?
Does the Act restrict your involvement in the campaign
regarding a possible referendum concerning the Hillside Initiative
Area including:
a.
Communicating with the press (including
letters to the editor)
and public?
b.
Speaking at referendum meetings?
c.
Fundraising for the referendum; signing
ballot arguments; campaigning for or against the
referendum?.
CONCLUSION 3.
The Act does not prevent your communication with the public
or press
(including letters to the editor) regarding a matter for
which you have a conflict of interest.
Consequently,
even though
you may not vote on issues relating to the Initiative Hillside
Area,
nor may you influence city councilmembers or staff,
you may
campaign on behalf of,
or in opposition to,
a new referendum.
Thus,
you are also permitted to speak at public meetings and
public forums on the subject.
In addition, you may sign ballot
arguments in favor of,
or in opposition to, the referendum,
and
send out mailings concerning its passage or defeat.
QUESTION 4.
Would the “public generally” exception apply so that you
could participate in the decisions listed in Question 1,
above?
CONCLUSION 4.
-
The “public generally” exception may be applicable to some of
the decisions.
However, the application of the exception must be
determined on the basis of specific facts on a case—by—case basis.
-
FACTS
-
-
In 1993,
a citizen’s group in the City of Loma Linda placed
an initiative on the ballot concerning specific hillside
development standards in an area known as the Hillside Initiative
Area, which encompasses the entire southern border of the city.
You were active in citizen groups supporting the Hillside
Initiative Measure, which provided for moderate development of the
area.
You are a now a councilmember for the City of Loma Linda.
Your personal residence is within 300 feet of the Hillside
Initiative Area.
You have a five person council in a small town
of 20,000 people.


Our File No.
1-95-229
Page 4
Developers and large land owners have been unsuccessful in
their legal challenges of the initiative measure.
They have asked
the council to consider minor to major changes to the initiative
measure.
You anticipate that the city council will be involved in
decisions concerning implementation standards which will require a
4/5 vote of the council.
Other decisions will involve:
(1)
legal
disputes regarding interpretation of the initiative’s language
with respect to existing lot standards and traffic to another
city,
(2) possible modification or overturning of the initiative
by city ballot,
(3)
zoning changes,
and
(4)
changes in the general
plan for the Hillside Initiative Area.
You also expect to take
certain political positions in regards to hillside development.
ANALYSIS
I.
Conflicts of Interest
As we discussed in our prior letter, Brauer Advice Letter,
No.
1-95-152,
you are prohibited from participating in any
decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable
3 and material
financial effect on your real property interest.
(Sections 87100
and 87103.)
Your personal residence,
which is within 300 feet of
the Hillside Initiative Area, is the real property interest which
may disqualify you from participating in city council decisions.
(Section 87103 (b).)
We advised you that it would appear likely that the series of
extensive decisions you have mentioned will have a financial
effect on your real property interests.
(Brauer Advoce Letter,
supra.)
For example, it is foreseeable that decisions to litigate
whether the existing initiative should be implemented or rescinded
will have some effect on the value of your personal residence.
--This is so because the Hillside Initiative Area, according to your
facts, is designed to permit only moderate development.
-
We also advised you that you must determine if the
foreseeable effect on your real property is material.
The type of
decisions you have described appear to impact your property
indirectly.
-
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In
re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No.
1-95-229
Page 5
With respect to your residence which is within 300 feet of
the Hillside Initiative Area,
4 Regulation 18702.3 states that the
effect of a decision on the residence is material if there will be
any financial effect on the value of the real property.
Thus,
you
may not participate in any decision concerning the initiative area
unless it will have no financial effect on your real property.
The Commission cannot determine whether there will be a
material financial effect on your property.
Ultimately, it is
your responsibility to ensure that the requirements of the
Commission’s materiality regulations aremet.
Thus, it is to your
benefit that a thorough assessment of financial effects is made
and that the facts and analysis on which the assessment is based,
including any appraisals,
5 are accurate and thoroughly documented.
(Hicks Advice Letter,
No.
A-92—492.)
We now proceed to your specific questions.
Litigation Decisions:
You have not provided information as to the
precise nature of the litigation decisions which will be before
the council.
However, you stated in our telephone conversation of
July 25,
1995, that you do not expect to be individually a named
party to any lawsuit.
Thus,
on the basis of these facts, whether
or not the effect would be material would have to be judged from
the perspective of your real property interests.
For example,
a
purely ministeria1 or procedural decision,
such as stipulating to
a date for a hearing, would probably not have a material financial
•effect on your real property or other economic interests.
However, the litigation decisions may affect the value of
your property.
Since there could be so many permutations of
litigation,
such as settlement terms, without specific facts, it
is not possible to determine with precision whether such decisions
will have a material financial effect on yoiar real property
interests.
- However,
as noted,
financial eff-ect on your
property would result in your disqualification.
Decisions Pertaining to Implementation of the Hillside Initiative
Measure and Possible Referendum to Revise the Existing Measure:
The analysis regarding decisions relating to implementation
of the disputed measure and revisions in a new referendum is
-
identical to the analysis that pertains to the litigation
decisions.
This would include a vote regarding the possible
placement of a new referendum on the ballot and decisions
concerning grading and building standards.
If it is reasonably
foreseeable that y of the governmental decisions will have any
Also as noted, each governmental decision is analyzed
independently to determine if there will be a foreseeable material
financial effect on a public official’s interest.
Please note that we are in no position to evaluate the accuracy
of any appraisals.
(Diaz Advice Letter,
No.
A—95—143.)


Our File No.
1-95—229
Page
6
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally,
on your real property interest, your disqualification
is required.
A.
Public Generally
Public officials with financial interests that will be
materially affected by a decision may still participate in the
decision if the effect on their property is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.
For the “public
generally” exception to apply,
a decision must affect the
official’s interests in substantially the same manner as it would
affect a significant segment of the public.’ (Regulation 18703.)6
Pursuant to Regulation 18703(a) (2), if you are affected in
substantially the same manner as a significant segment of the
population,
as defined in the regulation,
you may participate in
the decisiori.V
Regulation 18703 (a) (1) provides in pertinent part:
(1)
significant Segment:
The
governmental decision will affect a
“significant segment” of the public generally
as set forth below:
V
(A)
The decision will affect:
(i)
Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents,
or
(ii)
Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners,
or all
households in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency orthe district the
official represents, or
****
-
(B)
The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,
You have indicated that the City of Loma Linda has 20,000
residents.
You have provided no facts indicating the nuitiber of
homeowners, real property owners, households,
or individuals that
would be affected by the decisions.
However, if 5,000 individuals
would be affected in the same manner as you,
you could participate
in the decisions.
Or, for example, if there are 10,000 households
in the jurisdiction and 1,000
(10%)
households would be affected
6
Copies of the noted regulations have been previously provided
to you.


Our File No.
1—95—229
Page
7
by a decision in the same manner as you,
you could participate in
the decision.
A determination would have to be made on a case—by—case basis
with reference to specific decisions and their effect upon your
economic interests.
II.
Participation in Governmental Decisions
You indicate in your letter that you are confused as to how
to proceed on specific issues, particularly where you hold a
particular political view.
You ask for further guidance as to how
to proceed with respect to your official status.
As noted in our prior advice letter,
you are prohibited from
making, participating in making, or in any way attempting to use
your official position to influence a governmental decision in
which you know or have reason to know you have a financial
interest.
(Regulations 18700 and 18700.1.)
Pursuant to Regulation 18700.1(a),
you are prohibited from
influencing the city council and the planning commission,
3 and,
pursuant to Regulation 18700(c),
any other agency in your official
capacity.
Thus, for example, if you had a conflict of interest by
virtue of your real property interest,
you would be prohibited
from voting or discussing, making a recommendation to, or making
any written or oral contact with any official of your agency or an
agency under the city’s budgetary control to discuss the matter
for any purpose.
However,
you may address the city council or the
planning commission during an open meeting, etc., where you are
solely representing your personal interests as discussed below.
A.
Representing Your Own Interests Before Your Agency
Regulation 18700.1(b)
expressly provides an exception where
--
an otherwise disqualified official appears in the same manner as
any other member of the general public before the official’s own
agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function
represent the official’s
own personal interests in real property.
Where the exception does apply,
your comments must be limited
to your personal interests,
and care should be taken to clarify
that you are not acting in any official capacity.
(Larsen Advice
Letter,
No. A-87-15l..)
Thus, the exception would not permit you
to appear before the planning commission, or the city, to
represent any other person’s interests or the interests of any
group.
Presumably, the planning commission is under the budgetary
control of the city.


Our File No.
1-95—229
Page
8
B.
Communication with the Public or the Press
In addition, Regulation 18700.1(b) (2) provides that when you
communicate with the general public or the press (including
letters to the editor),
such communications regarding a matter in
which you have a conflict of interest are not considered an
attempt to use your position to influence a governmental decision,
whether you speak regarding your own interests or the interests of
others.
(Gallagher Advice Letter,
No.
1—94-279.)
Consequently, while you may not vote on issues relating to
the Hillside Initiative Area, nor may you influence city
councilmembers or staff,
you may campaign in your private capacity
with personal funds
7 for the referendum.
Thus, you are also
permitted to speak at public meetings on the subject and public
forums not sponsored by your agency.
In addition, you may sign
ballot arguments in favor of the referendum and send out mailings
concerning its passage.
8
Please note,
however, that your involvement with a committee
organized to support or oppose the measure may result in that
committee being your candidate controlled committee.
A candidate
may have only one campaign bank account and one controlled
committee for each campaign.
(Riddle Advice Letter,
No.
A—88—409.)
C.
Obtaining Information
The Act does not prohibit you from cbtaining information
otherwise available to the public.
You would, however,
be
prohibited from obtaining closed session materials regarding the
matter,
should there be such materials.
9
Other contacts with
respect to a decision in which you have a conflict of interest
should be avoided since it may be construed to be for the purpose
of influencing the decision.
-
-
-
III.
Segmentation and Legally Required Participation
If you are disqualified from participating in certain
decisions,
you may, nevertheless,
be able to participate in
certain aspects of the discussions and decisions if you are able
to segregate the decisions and follow the procedure described in
our prior advice letter.
Finally, Section 87101 permits an
official who is otherwise disqualified from participation under
The California Penal Code prohibits use of public funds for
campaign purposes.
(See Penal Code Section 424; Stanson v. Mott
(1976)
17 Cal.3d 206; Regulation 18420.)
8
Campaign funds may be used for this purpose and the
expenditures must be disclosed on your campaign reports.
See, Hamilton v.
City of Los Gatos
(1989)
213 Cal.App.3d 1050.


Our File No.
1-95-229
Page
9
Section 87100 to make or participate in the making of a
governmental decision when the official’s participation is legally
required.
(Regulation 18701.)
This may be the case with respect
to implementation decisions which require a 4/5 vote of the city.
We hope this letter has been helpful to you.
If you need
further assistance, please provide us with facts concerning the
financial effect of the specific decisions on your real property
interests.
For example, facts concerning the financial effect of
a decision on the value of your personal residence and facts
concerning the possible application of the “public generally”
exception.
We may be contacted at
(916) 322
_
5660
•1u
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
10
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page.

and public?

ballot arguments; campaigning for or against the
referendum?.

The Act does not prevent your communication with the public
or press
(including letters to the editor) regarding a matter for
which you have a conflict of interest.

you may not vote on issues relating to the Initiative Hillside
Area,

you may
campaign on behalf of,

a new referendum.

public forums on the subject.

or in opposition to, the referendum,
and
send out mailings concerning its passage or defeat.

you could participate in
the decision.
A determination would have to be made on a case—by—case basis
with reference to specific decisions and their effect upon your
economic interests.
II.
Participation in Governmental Decisions
You indicate in your letter that you are confused as to how
to proceed on specific issues, particularly where you hold a
particular political view.
You ask for further guidance as to how
to proceed with respect to your official status.
As noted in our prior advice letter,
you are prohibited from
making, participating in making, or in any way attempting to use
your official position to influence a governmental decision in
which you know or have reason to know you have a financial
interest.
(Regulations 18700 and 18700.1.)
Pursuant to Regulation 18700.1(a),
you are prohibited from
influencing the city council and the planning commission,
3 and,
pursuant to Regulation 18700(c),
any other agency in your official
capacity.
Thus, for example, if you had a conflict of interest by
virtue of your real property interest,
you would be prohibited
from voting or discussing, making a recommendation to, or making
any written or oral contact with any official of your agency or an
agency under the city’s budgetary control to discuss the matter
for any purpose.
However,
you may address the city council or the
planning commission during an open meeting, etc., where you are
solely representing your personal interests as discussed below.
A.
Representing Your Own Interests Before Your Agency
Regulation 18700.1(b)
expressly provides an exception where
--
an otherwise disqualified official appears in the same manner as
any other member of the general public before the official’s own
agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function
represent the official’s
own personal interests in real property.
Where the exception does apply,
your comments must be limited
to your personal interests,
and care should be taken to clarify
that you are not acting in any official capacity.
(Larsen Advice
Letter,
No. A-87-15l..)
Thus, the exception would not permit you
to appear before the planning commission, or the city, to
represent any other person’s interests or the interests of any
group.
Presumably, the planning commission is under the budgetary
control of the city.


Our File No.
1-95—229
B.
Communication with the Public or the Press
In addition, Regulation 18700.1(b) (2) provides that when you
communicate with the general public or the press (including
letters to the editor),
such communications regarding a matter in
which you have a conflict of interest are not considered an
attempt to use your position to influence a governmental decision,
whether you speak regarding your own interests or the interests of
others.
(Gallagher Advice Letter,
No.
1—94-279.)
Consequently, while you may not vote on issues relating to
the Hillside Initiative Area, nor may you influence city
councilmembers or staff,
you may campaign in your private capacity
with personal funds
7 for the referendum.
Thus, you are also
permitted to speak at public meetings on the subject and public
forums not sponsored by your agency.
In addition, you may sign
ballot arguments in favor of the referendum and send out mailings
concerning its passage.
8
Please note,
however, that your involvement with a committee
organized to support or oppose the measure may result in that
committee being your candidate controlled committee.
A candidate
may have only one campaign bank account and one controlled
committee for each campaign.
(Riddle Advice Letter,
No.
A—88—409.)
C.
Obtaining Information
The Act does not prohibit you from cbtaining information
otherwise available to the public.
You would, however,
be
prohibited from obtaining closed session materials regarding the
matter,
should there be such materials.
9
Other contacts with
respect to a decision in which you have a conflict of interest
should be avoided since it may be construed to be for the purpose
of influencing the decision.
-
-
-
III.
Segmentation and Legally Required Participation
If you are disqualified from participating in certain
decisions,
you may, nevertheless,
be able to participate in
certain aspects of the discussions and decisions if you are able
to segregate the decisions and follow the procedure described in
our prior advice letter.
Finally, Section 87101 permits an
official who is otherwise disqualified from participation under
The California Penal Code prohibits use of public funds for
campaign purposes.
(See Penal Code Section 424; Stanson v. Mott
(1976)
17 Cal.3d 206; Regulation 18420.)
8
Campaign funds may be used for this purpose and the
expenditures must be disclosed on your campaign reports.
See, Hamilton v.
City of Los Gatos
(1989)
213 Cal.App.3d 1050.


Our Page
9
Section 87100 to make or participate in the making of a
governmental decision when the official’s participation is legally
required.
(Regulation 18701.)
This may be the case with respect
to implementation decisions which require a 4/5 vote of the city.
We hope this letter has been helpful to you.
If you need
further assistance, please provide us with facts concerning the
financial effect of the specific decisions on your real property
interests.
For example, facts concerning the financial effect of
a decision on the value of your personal residence and facts
concerning the possible application of the “public generally”
exception.
We may be contacted at
(916) 322
_
5660
•1u

QUESTION: By virtue of your personal residence that is located within
300 feet of the boundaries of the Hillside Initiative Area,
do you
— 18995.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

(916) 322-5660


Our Page2
have a conflict of interest with respect to the following
decisions of the Loma Linda City Council:
a.
Discussion concerning litigation .against
the city that has been filed alleging damages due
to the current hillside initiative?
b.
Decisions concerning possible changes to
the Hillside Initiative, including the vote on the
placement of a new referendum on the ballot to amend
or rescind the existing initiative?
c.
Decisions to implement specific grading
and building standards related to the current
initiative which require a 4/5 vote of the city?

CONCLUSION: a.,
b
and c.
Because your property is within 300 feet of
the proposed Hillside Initiative Area,
you may not participate in
decision concerning the Hillside Initiative Area, unless it
will have no financial effect on your real property.
This would
include decisions concerning litigation on the existing
initiative, decisions concerning a possible referendum,
and
implementation decisions.
Generally, each decision should be
analyzed separately to determine if you have a conflict of
interest.
QUESTION 2.
Does it make a difference if you are only voting on
recommendations by the city’s planning commission to the City of
Loma Linda?
CONCLUSION 2.
-
-
No.
If you have a conflict of interest in a decision, you
are prohibited from using your official position with the city to
influence planning commission and city decisions.
You are
prohibited from participating or influencing the decision in city
council metings, for example,
no matter how th issue is raised.
Moreover,
you may not contact any members or employees of the
city, including the planning commission, to influence the
decision.
However,
you may address the city council with respect to
your personal interests.
Where this exception does apply, your
comments must be limited to your personal interests,
and care
should be taken to clarify that. you are not acting in any official
capacity.
Thus, for example, the exception would not permit you
to appear before the city council to represent any other person’s
interests or the interests of any group.


Our Page
3
QUESTION 3.
May.you express your views or address the public on your
views?
Does the Act restrict your involvement in the campaign
regarding a possible referendum concerning the Hillside Initiative
Area including:
a.
Communicating with the press (including
letters to the editor)
and public?
b.
Speaking at referendum meetings?
c.
Fundraising for the referendum; signing
ballot arguments; campaigning for or against the
referendum?.
CONCLUSION 3.
The Act does not prevent your communication with the public
or press
(including letters to the editor) regarding a matter for
which you have a conflict of interest.
Consequently,
even though
you may not vote on issues relating to the Initiative Hillside
Area,
nor may you influence city councilmembers or staff,
you may
campaign on behalf of,
or in opposition to,
a new referendum.
Thus,
you are also permitted to speak at public meetings and
public forums on the subject.
In addition, you may sign ballot
arguments in favor of,
or in opposition to, the referendum,
and
send out mailings concerning its passage or defeat.
QUESTION 4.
Would the “public generally” exception apply so that you
could participate in the decisions listed in Question 1,
above?
CONCLUSION 4.
-
The “public generally” exception may be applicable to some of
the decisions.
However, the application of the exception must be
determined on the basis of specific facts on a case—by—case basis.
-

Accordingly, we are treating your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329, and offer you only general guidance to your questions. 2 QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS QUESTION 1. Decisions concerning possible changes to the Hillside Initiative, including the vote on the placement of a new referendum on the ballot to amend or rescind the existing initiative? c.
